We should not use previously used commands: cat, authselect check, echo root... chpasswd, grep.

We can still use read_file to read /etc/pam.d/password-auth. Then detect lines with pam_unix.so usage. We can write back new content.

We can backup file using cp. But we can use run_cmd: "cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.bak". This is allowed.

Next we need to check if file exists. We can read it via read_file anyway.

We need to modify lines: For each line that contains "pam_unix.so" and not commented (#). We should modify to ensure rounds=5000 appended. If rounds= exists with any value not 5000, we should replace it with rounds=5000.

Thus sed command: For lines that contain "pam_unix.so" and not starting with #, we can modify using sed. But we need to escape spaces and slashes. Construct: sed -i '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ s/\(rounds=[^[:space:]]*\)/rounds=5000/' file to replace if rounds present; then sed -i '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ s/\([^[:space:]]\{1\}\)$/\1 rounds=5000/' to add rounds=5000 if not present. But easier: One sed that uses regex to find optionally existing rounds= but replace and add if missing: sed -i -e '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ s/\(rounds=[^[:space:]]*\)//g' file -e '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ s/$/ rounds=5000/'? But that would remove existing rounds=. We need to replace existing rounds with 5000. Might use: sed -i -e '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ { s/rounds=[^[:space:]]*/rounds=5000/; s/\([^[:space:]]\)$/\1 rounds=5000/ }' file

But care: The second substitution triggers even if rounds already present? Should be fine.

Alternatively, two separate commands: first replace existing rounds with 5000, then append if missing.

1. Replace: sed -i '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ s/rounds=[^[:space:]]*/rounds=5000/g' file
2. Append: sed -i '/^[^#]*pam_unix.so/ /rounds=/! s/$/ rounds=5000/' file
